food_name: Pomelo, fresh flesh (Citrus maxima, white or pink variety)

local_names.romanized_local: Yòu Zi (Mandarin); Yau (Cantonese); Limau Bali (Malay); Som-O (Thai); Bưởi (Vietnamese)

region_of_origin: Native to Southeast Asia; cultivated in Malaysia, Thailand, China, Vietnam, and the Philippines
food_subgroup: Fresh citrus fruit — large, thick-rinded citrus with juicy white or pink flesh segments; pith discarded before eating

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a dedicated term for Citrus maxima (pomelo). Closest parent is citrus fruit. No URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No confirmed SNOMED CT code for pomelo found in available reference; null assigned to avoid fabrication.

serving.description: 1 cup flesh (~190 g) — approximately one-quarter of a medium pomelo, the typical individual Chinese New Year serving
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 1 cup of peeled pomelo flesh (approximately 190 g), equivalent to roughly one-quarter of a medium pomelo as commonly consumed at Chinese New Year in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Pomelo — Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo) cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo) and FAO/INFOODS SEA
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for pomelo (Citrus maxima), cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition table. Both are approved primary regional sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Pomelo / Citrus maxima — University of Sydney GI Database reference for pomelo and closely related citrus (grapefruit GI ~25)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of ~25 (low) is referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database for pomelo/grapefruit. Pomelo is the progenitor of grapefruit and shares closely similar carbohydrate composition and fibre content. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (25 × 18.24) / 100 ≈ 4.6. Carbohydrate per 190 g serving = 9.6 × 1.9 = 18.24 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (2 mg/100 g), good potassium (216 mg/100 g), high Vitamin C; however, pomelo contains furanocoumarins (bergamottin, dihydroxybergamottin) that inhibit intestinal CYP3A4 — the same mechanism as grapefruit — and can significantly raise plasma levels of amlodipine (calcium channel blocker), statins, and other CYP3A4-metabolised antihypertensive drugs
hypertension.guidance: Pomelo is nutritionally beneficial for blood pressure due to its low sodium and good potassium content, but patients taking amlodipine, atorvastatin, simvastatin, or other CYP3A4-metabolised medications should be counselled to avoid or limit pomelo consumption exactly as they would grapefruit, as furanocoumarins in pomelo can substantially increase drug plasma concentrations and risk of adverse effects.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~25) and low glycemic load per cup serving (~4.6); naringenin flavonoid may additionally support insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pomelo is one of the most appropriate citrus fruits for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, with a GI of approximately 25 and a GL of only 4.6 per 190 g cup serving; it can be consumed freely within a balanced diet, and its naringenin content may provide secondary insulin-sensitising benefit, though patients on CYP3A4-metabolised diabetes medications should be counselled regarding the drug interaction risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero cholesterol, negligible saturated fat (0 g/100 g), high Vitamin C (61 mg/100 g), good potassium (216 mg/100 g), and naringenin/naringin flavonoids with demonstrated anti-inflammatory and lipid-modulating properties; CYP3A4 drug interaction risk relevant for patients on statins
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pomelo is a heart-healthy fruit with zero cholesterol, negligible saturated fat, and high Vitamin C and potassium; however, patients with cardiovascular disease who are prescribed statins (atorvastatin, simvastatin) or other CYP3A4-metabolised cardiovascular drugs must be advised to treat pomelo with the same caution as grapefruit due to its furanocoumarin-mediated CYP3A4 inhibition.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High Vitamin C (61 mg/100 g; 116 mg per 190 g serving) and naringenin flavonoid with demonstrated anti-inflammatory activity (inhibits TNF-alpha, IL-6)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pomelo provides excellent Vitamin C (approximately 116 mg per cup serving, well above the RDA) and naringenin, which has demonstrated anti-inflammatory activity in vitro and in observational studies; it is a suitable fruit for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pomelo (柚子, Yòu Zi; Malay: Limau Bali; Tamil: பம்பரமாஸ் / சாத்துக்குடி; Hindi: चकोतरा; Thai: ส้மโอ; Vietnamese: Bưởi) is the world's largest citrus fruit and the ancestral progenitor of the modern grapefruit (a pomelo × sweet orange hybrid). Native to Southeast Asia and widely cultivated in Malaysia, Thailand, China, and Vietnam, pomelo is deeply embedded in Chinese New Year (CNY) culture in Singapore: the Cantonese pronunciation 柚 (yau) is homophonous with 有 (yau = to have/possess), making pomelo a powerful symbol of abundance and prosperity. In Singapore, pomelo is sold at wet markets and supermarkets year-round but peaks dramatically during CNY. The fruit is characterised by its enormous size, very thick pale-yellow to pink pith (which is discarded), and large, moist, grapefruit-like flesh segments that are sweet-tart and juicy. The flesh may be white or pink depending on variety. Nutritionally, pomelo is exceptional: it is very low in calories (38 kcal/100 g), has one of the lowest glycemic indices of all tropical fruits (~25, low GI), and delivers a negligible glycemic load of only 4.6 per standard cup serving (190 g). It is rich in Vitamin C (61 mg/100 g; approximately 116 mg per cup serving), provides useful potassium (216 mg/100 g), and contains the flavanone naringenin, which has demonstrated insulin-sensitising and anti-inflammatory properties in research settings. The critical clinical concern for pomelo — identical to grapefruit — is its content of furanocoumarins (bergamottin, dihydroxybergamottin), which inhibit intestinal CYP3A4 enzymes and can substantially increase plasma concentrations of amlodipine (first-line antihypertensive in Singapore), statins (atorvastatin, simvastatin), and other CYP3A4-metabolised medications. Patients on these drugs must be counselled regarding pomelo exactly as they would be for grapefruit.
common_user_queries: is pomelo good for high blood pressure

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (primary approved regional source) cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition table. GI value referenced from University of Sydney GI Database for pomelo/grapefruit. All values are consistent across sources. Food identity is unambiguous.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for pomelo flesh not reported in HPB Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables as of April 2026; total fat is very low (0.1 g/100 g) making this clinically negligible. water_g set to -1: moisture content not explicitly reported in the primary sources consulted (HPB Foodo, FAO/INFOODS SEA); pomelo flesh is approximately 89% water by general citrus reference but this value is not confirmed in the named approved sources and is therefore not entered. vitamin_b12_mcg and vitamin_d_mcg are 0.0: confirmed zero for fresh citrus fruit — pomelo is a plant food and naturally contains no vitamin B12 or vitamin D; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo and FAO/INFOODS SEA. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as pomelo is a plant food. saturated_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per HPB Foodo. trans_fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero in unprocessed fresh fruit. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database reference for pomelo and grapefruit (pomelo's hybrid descendant); pomelo and grapefruit share near-identical carbohydrate profiles and fibre content, making this a defensible analogous estimate. GL per serving = (25 × 18.24) / 100 = 4.6, where 18.24 g carbohydrate = 9.6 g/100 g × 190 g serving. CYP3A4 drug interaction warning (furanocoumarins) is clinically significant and documented in health_context for hypertension and cardiovascular disease conditions. q8 fix (iteration 3): performed exhaustive self-check of aliases_by_language vs flat aliases array. All strings present in aliases_by_language['english'], ['chinese'], ['malay'], ['tamil'], ['hindi'], ['thai'], ['vietnamese'], and ['romanized'] verified verbatim in flat aliases array. Added 'பம்பரமாஸ் / சாத்துக்குடி' (without parenthetical), 'Yau', and 'Som-O' to flat aliases to ensure complete coverage of all romanized entries.
